conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you through this process accurately.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience! Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. 

To get started, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child I'm responsible for. I'm inquiring about my son Alex - he's 18 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you're inquiring about your son Alex who is 18 years old and lives with you.

Since Alex is over 16, his eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he's in approved education or training. Could you please tell me: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he studying at school, college, or in an approved training program?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school and hasn't started anything since then.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to help me assess Alex's eligibility properly.

**When did Alex finish school or leave his education/training?** (Approximately what month and year?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He finished school in June, so just a few months ago.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. One more important question:

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving school in June?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or the armed forces.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also check a few final details to give you a complete assessment:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own right** (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working 24 hours or more a week, and he's not claiming any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me explain the situation:

According to the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16-19, you can still receive Child Benefit **if** they stay in approved education or training.

There is a provision that allows Child Benefit to continue for **20 weeks** if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training **and** registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

In Alex's case:
- He is 18 years old
- He left school in June (a few months ago)
- He is not currently in approved education or training
- He has not registered with a careers service or the armed forces
- He is not working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in his own right

Unfortunately, since Alex is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed forces, you would not be eligible to receive Child Benefit for him at this time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**However**, if Alex were to start approved education or training in the future, you could potentially become eligible again. If you have any questions or if Alex's circumstances change, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and left school in June (a few months ago). Under UK Child Benefit rules, for children aged 16-19, the claimant can only receive Child Benefit if the child stays in approved education or training. There is an exception allowing Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Alex has not registered with either. Since Alex is not in approved education or training, has not registered with a careers service or armed forces, is not working 24+ hours per week, and is not claiming benefits in his own right, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old and lives at home. Alex left school in June and is not currently in approved education or training. He has not registered with a careers service or the armed forces, which would have allowed a 20-week extension for 16-17 year olds. As Alex does not meet the requirements for Child Benefit eligibility for children over 16, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

Under UK Child Benefit rules, for children aged 16-19, the claimant can only receive Child Benefit if the child stays in approved education or training. There is an exception allowing Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Alex has not registered with either. Since Alex is not in approved education or training, has not registered with a careers service or armed forces, is not working 24+ hours per week, and is not claiming benefits in his own right, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.

He has not registered with a careers service or the armed forces, which would have allowed a 20-week extension for 16-17 year olds. As Alex does not meet the requirements for Child Benefit eligibility for children over 16, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
